---
title: "Proofs"
---

## Why This Module Matters

Proof writing is the point where math stops being a pile of techniques and starts becoming a language.

If you want to read theory-heavy papers well, this module is not optional.

::: {.meta-strip}
::: {.meta-box}
**Prerequisites**
Algebra fluency and patience
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Unlocks**
Logic, discrete math, real analysis, learning theory
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Research Use**
Theorem reading, assumption tracking, proof debugging
:::
:::

## Module Sequence

1. [Statements and Quantifiers](concepts/statements-and-quantifiers.qmd)
2. [Direct Proof](concepts/direct-proof.qmd)
3. Contrapositive and contradiction
4. Induction
5. Counterexamples and proof debugging

## Navigation Rule

The global sidebar shows only the proof `spine`:

- the module overview
- the core concept pages you should study in order

When this module grows, worked examples, exercises, and paper-bridge pages should live inside the relevant topic package or on the module page, not all at once in the global sidebar.

## What You Should Be Able To Do After This Module

- read a theorem statement carefully
- identify hidden quantifiers
- choose a reasonable proof strategy
- explain why a proof step is valid
- write short proofs without collapsing into symbol soup

## Paper Bridge

The first thing proofs teach you for research is how to read assumptions without skipping them. That single habit affects optimization, probability, learning theory, and statistics almost immediately.
